summary: American poet associated with the collection Leaves of Grass, which contains poems like 'Song of Myself,' 'Crossing Brooklyn Ferry,' 'I Sing the Body Electric,' 'O Captain! My Captain!,' and 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd.' Known for his celebration of democracy, the body, and comradeship, and for his influence on later poets including Allen Ginsberg and Latin American modernists.

works.name: Song of Myself
works.description: The centerpiece of Leaves of Grass, this long poem is Whitman's most frequently clued work. Questions focus on its celebration of the self, its democratic vision equating all people, and its famously expansive catalogues. The speaker 'loafes' and observes grass, contains 'multitudes,' sounds a 'barbaric yawp,' and interprets grass as a 'uniform hieroglyphic' and 'the handkerchief of the Lord.' The eleventh section describing twenty-eight young men bathing is a particularly notable episode.

works.name: Leaves of Grass
works.description: Whitman's magnum opus, a poetry collection he revised throughout his life, ending with the 'deathbed' edition completed in Camden. Clues reference its homoerotic 'Calamus' poems, its first edition of 12 poems, and the fact that Emily Dickinson claimed not to have read it because she 'was told that it was disgraceful.' The collection also contains 'I Sing the Body Electric,' the 'Sea Drift' section, and 'Drum-Taps.'

works.name: When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd
works.description: Whitman's elegy for Abraham Lincoln, one of his most clued individual poems. Questions focus on its imagery of a hermit thrush singing, a 'great star' drooping in the western sky (allegorizing Lincoln), and the 'Song of the bleeding throat.' The poem is part of the 'Memories of Abraham Lincoln' cluster and the 'Sequel to Drum-Taps.'

works.name: O Captain! My Captain!
works.description: Whitman's most famous Lincoln elegy, frequently used as the ultimate giveaway clue. The poem describes the ship anchored 'safe and sound,' the captain 'fallen cold and dead,' and is consistently paired with 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' as Lincoln elegies.

works.name: Crossing Brooklyn Ferry
works.description: A frequently clued poem that opens 'Flood-tide below me! I watch you face to face' and describes 'Crowds of men and women attired in the usual costumes.' The poem ends by claiming 'you furnish your parts toward the soul.' It is set on the East River ferry and celebrates the shared experience of commuters across time.

works.name: Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking
works.description: A poem about a mockingbird calling for its lost mate, featuring the 'musical shuttle' and 'the low and delicious word death.' The phrase 'the sea whisper'd me' ends the poem. It is part of the 'Sea Drift' section of Leaves of Grass and was set to music by Frederick Delius.

works.name: I Sing the Body Electric
works.description: A poem celebrating the human body, containing 'the armies of those I love engirth me and I engirth them' and a famous catalogue of body parts ending with 'O I say now these are the soul!' The poem also asserts that 'the expression of a well-made man appears not only in his face.'

works.name: A Supermarket in California (Ginsberg)
works.description: Allen Ginsberg's poem addressed to Whitman is one of the most frequently clued cross-references. Whitman appears as a 'childless, lonely old grubber, poking among the meats in the refrigerator and eyeing the grocery boys,' and Ginsberg asks him 'Which way does your beard point tonight?' and 'Who killed the pork chops? What price bananas?'

clues.clue: Ginsberg imagines Whitman 'poking among the meats' and 'eyeing the grocery boys'

works.name: The Sleepers
works.description: A dream-vision poem featuring a 'beautiful gigantic swimmer' dashed against rocks, George Washington weeping over the Battle of Brooklyn, and a Native American woman visiting the speaker's mother. Opens with the speaker wandering 'all night in my vision.'

works.name: I Hear America Singing
works.description: A short poem describing the 'varied carols' of American workers -- mechanics, carpenters, boatmen, shoemakers, and ploughboys -- each singing 'what belongs to him or her.' Often paired with Langston Hughes's 'I, Too, Sing America' as a response.

clues.clue: Hughes's 'I, Too, Sing America' is a response to this poem

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Whitman's biographical details that appear in clues include his residence in Camden, New Jersey; his companion Peter Doyle (coded as '16.4' in notebooks); his use of phrenological terms 'amativeness' and 'adhesiveness'; his experimental memoir Specimen Days; his early temperance novel Franklin Evans; and his wide influence on later poets. He was popularized in Latin America by an 1887 Jose Marti essay, and influenced Hart Crane, Ezra Pound, and the Latin American modernists Ruben Dario and Pablo Neruda.

works.name: Other Poems
works.description: Several other poems appear with some frequency: 'A Noiseless Patient Spider' (the soul compared to a spider launching 'filament, filament, filament'); 'On the Beach at Night' (a father tells his daughter 'Weep not, child' while observing Jupiter and the Pleiades); 'The Wound-Dresser' (Civil War nursing, 'Many a soldier's kiss dwells on these bearded lips'); 'Song of the Open Road' ('Afoot and light-hearted'); 'Drum-Taps' (Civil War collection); and 'O Pioneers!' (the Willa Cather novel takes its title from a Whitman poem).

works.name: Musical Settings
works.description: Whitman's poetry has been extensively set to music. Ralph Vaughan Williams used his texts for A Sea Symphony (opening with 'Behold!' in B-flat minor); Gustav Holst wrote an overture and The Mystic Trumpeter; Paul Hindemith composed an oratorio When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd; George Walker won the 1996 Pulitzer for Lilacs (for voice and orchestra); and Frederick Delius set 'Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking' in 1906.

clues.clue: Vaughan Williams's A Sea Symphony sets his texts, opening with 'Behold!' brass fanfare

clues.clue: George Walker's Lilacs won 1996 Pulitzer, for voice and orchestra
examples: a 1996 Pulitzer Prize-winning piece for voice and orchestra named for a work by this man

comprehensive_summary:
Walt Whitman is one of the most heavily clued American poets in quizbowl, appearing across literature, fine arts, and occasionally geography and history categories. His magnum opus Leaves of Grass, which he revised throughout his life into a 'deathbed' edition completed in Camden, New Jersey, is the central work. Within it, 'Song of Myself' dominates with its celebration of democracy, the body, and the self -- the speaker 'loafes' and observes grass, interprets it as 'a uniform hieroglyphic' and 'the handkerchief of the Lord,' contains 'multitudes,' sounds a 'barbaric yawp,' and asks 'What is the grass?' The eleventh section's twenty-eight young men bathing, watched by a woman, is a frequently clued homoerotic episode.

Whitman's Lincoln elegies form another major cluster: 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd' features a hermit thrush singing a 'Song of the bleeding throat' and a 'great star' drooping in the western sky, while 'O Captain! My Captain!' describes the captain 'fallen cold and dead' after the ship is 'anchor'd safe and sound.' 'Crossing Brooklyn Ferry' opens with 'Flood-tide below me!' and describes commuters 'attired in the usual costumes.' 'Out of the Cradle Endlessly Rocking' features a mockingbird, 'the low and delicious word death,' and ends with 'the sea whisper'd me.'

The 'Calamus' poems celebrate 'the manly love of comrades' and are noted for their homoerotic content. Whitman used phrenological terms 'amativeness' and 'adhesiveness' to distinguish types of attraction, and coded his companion Peter Doyle as '16.4' in notebooks. Other frequently clued poems include 'I Sing the Body Electric' ('the armies of those I love engirth me'), 'A Noiseless Patient Spider' (the soul launching 'filament, filament, filament'), and 'I Hear America Singing' (the 'varied carols' of workers).

Whitman's cross-references are extensive. Allen Ginsberg's 'A Supermarket in California' imagines Whitman 'poking among the meats' and 'eyeing the grocery boys,' asking 'Which way does your beard point tonight?' Langston Hughes's 'I, Too, Sing America' responds to 'I Hear America Singing.' Federico Garcia Lorca wrote an ode to Whitman in Poet in New York, calling him 'Adam of blood, Macho.' Ruben Dario coined his Spanish nickname 'el gran viejo,' and Jose Marti's 1887 essay began his 'cult' among Latin American modernists. Hart Crane's The Bridge responds to Whitman especially in 'Cape Hatteras,' and Ezra Pound acknowledged his influence in 'A Pact.'

Whitman's poetry has been extensively set to music: Vaughan Williams's A Sea Symphony (B-flat minor brass fanfare on 'Behold!'), Hindemith's oratorio, George Walker's Pulitzer-winning Lilacs, Frederick Delius's 1906 setting of 'Out of the Cradle,' and Gustav Holst's overture and The Mystic Trumpeter.
